title: Watchdog eyes ALP ‘bias’ for industry super
full_text:
The Coalition has taken the extraordinary step of asking the National Anti-Corruption Commission to investigate the Albanese government’s relationship with the industry superannuation sector, accusing Labor of pressuring the corporate regulator to “suit their mates” and allow union-affiliated funds to buy more houses.
Opposition housing spokesman Andrew Bragg has written to the integrity watchdog asking it to investigate the influence of industry super funds on the government, raising concerns over the process that led to the Australian Securities & Investments Commission backflipping on its opposition to changing stamp duty reporting requirements by funds.
A spokesman for the NACC said the referral was under “active consideration”. Senator Bragg said it was a mistake to allow super funds to disclose stamp duty over seven years in fees and cost summaries rather than the previous one-year requirement. He said the new rule gave institutional buyers an advantage over mum-and-dad investors and people who use self-managed super funds.
He accused the government of pressuring ASIC to make the regulatory change, after Jim Chalmers was personally asked to act on stamp duty reporting requirements by CBUS and other industry super funds.
Senator Bragg told The Australian that the government’s relationship with industry super funds warranted further scrutiny “on the back of the crooked decision to cut SMSFs from investing in housing supply”. “This ban simply helps the big funds more and damages the interests of families and individuals,” he said. “This is a significant integrity issue for which the NACC was established. Jim Chalmers has spent years trying to enrich union/super interests. The government has now literally bent the disclosure rules to suit their mates so they can buy more houses. Thanks to him, Labor mates can cover up their stamp duty costs when they acquire homes.” A spokesman for the Treasurer dismissed the allegations levelled by Senator Bragg.
“This shadow minister hates superannuation and loves attention, and the Treasurer won’t be indulging him on either front or dignifying this rubbish with a response,” the spokesman said.
In his letter to the NACC, Senator Bragg provided a written timetable outlining what he argued was excessive influence on the government that influenced ASIC amending the regulation known as RG97. He wrote that the lobbying from the superannuation sector began months after the Albanese government was elected in 2022, at an investor roundtable that included senior officials from HESTA, Rest Super, CBUS Super, AustralianSuper, UniSuper, Colonial First State and Aware Super.
Senator Bragg noted that Dr Chalmers attempted to block the release of correspondence between his office and CBUS following the investor roundtable meeting, invoking “commercial in confidence” to block Freedom of Information applications and a Senate-authorised order for production of documents.
After the Office of the Australian Information Commissioner instructed the Treasurer to release correspondence between Cbus and his office, it emerged the Wayne Swan-chaired super fund argued the “requirement to disclose stamp duty as part of fees and costs is emerging as a disincentive to invest in Australian property and certain infrastructure”.
However, ASIC’s eventual regulatory change was not in line with what was being argued by CBUS. In its submission to a review into the regulation, CBUS argued that “averaging or smoothing stamp duty over a longer time horizon does not resolve the underlying issues”. Senator Bragg wrote that Dr Chalmers welcomed ASIC’s decision to review RG 97 after the 2025 economic reform roundtable, with the Treasurer saying he did not want unnecessary obstacles or impediments for superannuation investing in housing. He wrote that Freedom of Information documents showed ASIC was “resisting the changes prior to the review being announced”, but went on to amend the regulation less than a year later.
